title: 2025/08/14 EDSI
displayTitle: 2025-08-14 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority
description: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority Broadcasting Standards Authority Stacey Wood, Chief Executive Susie Staley, Chair
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority. Broadcasting Standards Authority. Stacey Wood, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Thanks for your patience. Uh, the earlier I outcome went longer than we thought. That means You can cross examinee us for less time. Quick fire. Quick fire. Are you feeling happy about that? Is it?

Thank you. Thank you so much. So now we have, um, yeah, um, uh, until, uh, two o'clock. And if you would like to start by introducing yourselves and then some opening remarks. If you could keep your opening remarks really, really brief, like around two minutes. And then we will have questions from members. Thank you. Thank you.

Um, good afternoon, Kira. I'm Susie Staley. I'm the chair of the Broadcasting Standards Authority and of course I have here my Chief executive Stacey Wood. And, uh, she will complete the opening remarks. Thank you Madam Chair. Thank you. Thank you. Sorry.

Under two minutes I'll do my best. Uh, Under two minutes. Under two minutes, yeah. In two minutes max. I know it's tough. Alright. It's been, uh, two and a half years since we last appeared in front of you. So I will just give a really quick, uh, refresher on who we are and what we do. Uh, the BSA is a independent crown entity, a PSI judicial tribunal.

Uh, we're governed by a four person board, chaired by Susie, uh, supported by eight staff, including me. So we are not quite the smallest crown entity, but we are pretty close. Um, we exist to uphold standards and broadcasting. Uh, so our jurisdiction is over programs. It's audio visual content transmitted to the public by any means of telecommunication.

And traditionally, this has meant terrestrial and satellite TV and radio. And our act specifically excludes on demand content, um, with the huge change to how people consume media. Since our act was passed in 1989, uh, it's no longer exactly clear where the edges of our jurisdiction are.

And we've had some advice that it may include online radio or TV stations, uh, but not the likes of Netflix, of course, because of the on demand exclusion with reg regulatory reform of our act being on and off the agenda in the last few years. And with our limited resources, we've held off on a full review of our complaints in maybe jurisdiction.

Uh, but we do need legislative clarity, uh, to bring our act into the present. And even our most traditional broadcasters are moving towards digital only offerings. So they need that clarity as well. Uh, our vision is freedom of expression without harm, uh, which is simple to say, but not always simple to give a feature.

Two. But I'll leave it there. Thank, thank you. And happy to answer any questions you have. Yeah. We'll start with Uben Davidson. Thank you. And thank you for that, um, for that opening. You, you mentioned in the, in your opening comments, um, alternative platforms for content. So we know more New Zealand as a consuming content through online platforms like social media.

You'd agree with that? Yes. Yeah. So that content currently sits outside of the BSA regulatory remit, but do you see the similarities between some of that content where perhaps on a platform you can find a 40 minute video, for example, on YouTube? Uh, it's the same format as a documentary or radio program.

Are you, you're finding this kind of thing across multiple platforms? Can I, uh, just before you answer, I know you can answer the question. Can I say that this is the, uh, review briefing on 20 23, 20 24, um, annual review of broadcasting. So if you want to bring your questions to the annual review, rather than having a general policy discussion or a senior opinions on that.

So if you bring it back to that, that would be fine. I'll let the question Go ahead. So evidenced in, in the review is that shift of viewership from, uh, traditional terrestrial or broadcast media into online platforms. Um, and on those online platforms, part of that shift is because the equivalent content now sits on those online platforms as opposed to in the broadcast ones that are measured here.

Um, so I, I guess the question here is, um, that shift, are you tracking actively where the, the platforms that are carrying that content? Yeah, we, we are not tracking it. Uh, however, we are conscious and we do have research and surveys, not just by ourselves, but by others that show where those audiences are accessing their content.

There is an argument for a platform platform agnostic approach to regulation where we are looking at who, who's producing the content rather than where are they seeing, where are people watching it. Mm-hmm. Uh, because we know there's, you know, there's a huge range of approaches to regulation of social media and it's quite a different concept to the regulation of, uh, broadcast content.

There's some different concepts of harm prevention and safety that you're trying to address. So it is not a one size fits all approach. Um, I don't know if that answers your question again. Can I add, can I add that? I mean, as you'll be aware, we're not a policy shop. Mm-hmm. We certainly keep an eye on with our resources and we only have certain amount of resources and we're conscious of using them correctly.

We give input to, as a, we hope an expert advisor to the, to the ministry on possible changes. Um, and it's fair to say that in this area, every jurisdiction, certainly democratic jurisdictions are struggling you to see what's happening in the uk uh, Australia and the eu.

So Stacey did join a group some years ago now, uh, and to Ireland to have a look at what they're doing. But nothing has particularly moved on in New Zealand. It, it is hard, and we are not really there at the moment. We, we do struggle sometimes with our definition of broadcasting, which is 35 years old. Mm-hmm.

Yeah. Quite a lot's changed in most days. Yes, it has. So, um, just, just one or two things. So a a furthest up on that I guess would be, given that you have a really, um, or you have a good understanding of what you are regulating, that doesn't necessarily mean that viewers do. So are you receiving complaints from people for content that in your regulatory framework isn't broadcasting?

Yeah, we, we regularly get inquiries and complaints from people about content that we can't or don't regulate or, and sometimes it's an easy matter of redirecting them to the media council or advertising standards authority. And other times it's saying, um, tell Facebook your problems. Um, we, and nobody has regulatory purview over that.

Yeah. And are you, are you measuring the, um, complaints that aren't covered? We measure them as a blog, um, but we don't track where everyone's going. So it's probably around a quarter to a fifth of inquiries that we get, uh, uh, fluctuates over time. People got, people get, um, interested in different issues.

Yeah. Uh, but that's, that's about it. You know, We, we have, it's a very interesting question 'cause I asked my chief executive the same, but we would say about 20% of our staff's time or is spent, uh, triaging and sort of arbitrage. We have a policy of no wrong door amongst the, uh, authorities. So it might just be say, well this is actually advertising standards.

But you're right. There's still a gap, uh, and sort of, uh, NetSafe of course, uh, but there isn't any other regulated that's dealing with the platforms for which you've referred. We, we also don't have a good idea of how many people would complain if they knew there was a complaints process to complain through. Yeah.

So just to up on that, because a lot of content that people discover online originated in a broadcast medium. So if someone wants to complain about a piece of content that they've seen on an unnamed platform and they come to you, but you know that it originate, do you redirect people to say, try re try resubmitting this complaint with the original broadcast details? Uh, Yeah. If they're able to provide the original broadcast details and the original broadcasters within our timeframe, then we can accept it.

Yeah. Uh, so if it's got a clear source on it, that's one of our broadcasters. If it says one news on it, uh, we can advise 'em to contact the broadcaster and, um, find out the original broadcast date. But if it's outside of that timeframe, then even though it's still accessible online, can no longer take a complaint about it. And just to be really clear on that, 'cause you're saying that's something you can do, but is that something that you proactively do?

Yep. Yep. Alright. Uh, new question from Linda. Yeah. It's a new question, but also Sort of a sub along the same lines as my colleague. 'cause I'm wondering about how, and it's great that you've said that no door is the wrong door mm-hmm. In this space. Um, and understanding, you know, that front facing customer service type approach, um, and the fact that there are some people that you know, really need that liaison.

Do you think that this continues to be a gap across, uh, regulators and in the complaints process? 'cause we've heard it in other spaces that, um, knowing fully what the brief is of each organization really helps place the complainant in the right space and with you in an ever changing environment. Um, you know, how, how does that work for you practically with staff who are on the phone or online trying to, um, divide up the work and the complaints that are coming through the door? Yeah.

Uh, we have good relationships with our co regulators and our different spaces. So where there's an issue, um, that we're identifying with people not coming to the right door, uh, we'll come up with ways to support them. So for instance, when it comes around to general election time, the electoral advertising and programming regulation rules are very fragmented and overlapping.

And so we put out, for example, joint comms with the electoral commission, the Media Council Advertising Standards Authority so that everyone knows who's doing what. Uh, we can inform watches and uh, direct them to the right place. All the while knowing that if it's advertising on social media, anything goes out. Yeah, Yeah, yeah. No, Just Stop on that.

Um, 'cause I, I like, I know in the report that we've received, like there's, I guess, um, differing views in terms of the, the quality of the handling of the complaints and, um, you know, some people are satisfied and not, um, what's your internal processes for evaluating how you can improve in the way that you handle complaints, but also, and it's, uh, customer satisfaction is something very different, but, you know, you're always trying to look for ways with which to standardize and improve Practice. Absolutely. So

part of our oversight of the standard system is, and our engagement functions is to check in with complainants, broadcasters and the public to check that we are getting things right. So we do the complainant survey in years, like the recent years where the vast majority, over 90% of complaints haven't been upheld, people are a bit more dissatisfied just with the process just because it hasn't given them the outcome that they wanted. Um, but, and they Feel free to tell us that.

But, but generally Language that I couldn't possibly Repeat, generally still happy with the process that they've experienced. Uh, we do litmus testing where we go out and get focus groups with the public on a, um, group of decisions on a topic or a standard and say, explain the reasoning and then say, do you agree? And generally those results are sort of around the 90% mark of people who, even if they would don't agree, they understand why a decision's been made the way it has.

So we have pretty robust evidence externally and internally that we're applying the standards consistently and clearly, and that our staff are treating people with care and respect. And we also do research with, in terms of, um, efficiency and costs. We are now move that to every two years, but a topic of, we get our complaints and it may be a particular area that we struggle with or that we had a lot of complaints in, uh, with that is reviewed.

Uh, it's a research piece of paper usually by an expert in the field. Mm-hmm. We've just recently done that. So, and then any learnings from that, uh, we, we then bring to the table when we are dealing with complaints. Thank you. Alright. Yeah. Thank you Madam Chair. Just going a bit further into the litmus testing that you, that you referenced there.

Um, and it's outlined in the report some of the previous areas of focus for that. Um, how do you identify those as areas of focus? Different ways? Uh, sometimes it's a rise in the number and complaints about a particular topic or under a different standard. Sometimes it's based on the authorities, um, Where we've struggled Yeah.

Where, where they've been complex or difficult and, and the world is moving on. Um, yep. So it, it, it is a, a slight finger in the air, but it's a, we review what we've done and it's always good to get somebody else also commenting on looking a hindsight review. So we don't just say, we are wonderful and we carry on doing what we're doing.

So it, there is some judgment in that, but it, it hasn't proved difficult to find topics that perhaps have been a bit naughty for us over the previous couple of years. Yeah. And do you have, have you identified, like, how, how do you project out that, um, the future work and, and how do you identify how far in advance you identify other issues, future research?

Uh, well, it's probably not so far in advance. When we look at our statement of intent and our, our commitment, it's what we say, well, what we have to do in hindsight, we say what has been, uh, difficult or complex over the last uh, period, and that's how we do it. And it's on a rolling basis. So it's different, different areas that we might let miss test. Sure.

Where we do look ahead though, is, uh, with our guidance and our yeah. Advisories, uh, where we can see that there's a number of complaints coming in on an issue, and we might want to give some guidance either to broadcasters or complainants about complaints that are likely or unlikely to succeed or care and reporting, then that's where we'll be a bit more broadly looking At. Yeah. And a concrete example of that is during COVID we all remember COVID, we've got a, a large number of complaints about everything.

And there's was a common theme that started to come through to, you know, well, well, the way through COVID that, you know, the COVID vaccination was unsafe. And, and we, and in the end we said, we are, we are wasting taxpayer's money here. We will not entertain in itself a complaint that just says because the Minister of Health or Health New Zealand deficit, we take it that it is safe within those boundaries that also then elicits a slight variation that it's not safe for pregnant women.

So, so that's moving forward and that helps the broadcasters as well because we are only seeing, you know, tip of the iceberg. They of course have their own internal complaints, um, mechanism and they have, they receive, receive volumes more than we do. That's right. Yeah. Before They, so you've, any other subry from you?

Not on me. Got, thank you. Um, I just wanted to dig into how the organization has changed, um, since previous years particularly. Um, it's, it's a useful discussion about what has changed since that pandemic period. And I imagine that you have made some changes around the way that you assess things like accuracy and the way that people's perception of truth and accuracy is being presented to you when you're complaining.

Um, but first can I ask you about the proposal that would've seen BSA administer and monitor the new fair bargaining framework for news media and digital platforms? Um, that was something that would've had a big impact on the organization. Um, it would've been a response to changes in the way your audiences consume content.

Um, so how, you know, how, how did the, did did the organization make organizational change? What are the transitional arrangements? How should we understand your governance arrangements as is moving on from there? Sorry, what Was that full For? Fair Digital? Oh yeah. Yeah. We, we haven't made any organizational changes.

Uh, the bill didn't progress mm-hmm. To a point where we needed to, uh, we did do sort of road mapping talking to overseas like bodies, um, to sort of see what, what would be needed. But, uh, with the sort of changing global circumstances and uncertainty as to whether it would go ahead, we, uh, stop short of any organizational change.

Okay. That's good. Mm-hmm. Um, no, no. Who, who has a question? Oh, just one more, um, just uh, on that. So with eight people sort of administering the complaints process, um, if we needed to build in that kind of framework and capacity to the organization, what, what expansion were you predicting in that year to, to now?

Like, what would have been the change? Oh, we'd want to quadruple in size. We don't, the answer is without having looked at what it would involve. Yeah. And we've talked to and what our role would be, uh, we wouldn't, we wouldn't immediately propose changes. 'cause essentially, you dunno what you don't know. Yeah.

And then it may be just with my business head on that you would contract, you know, to, to, to establish, uh, and, and any regulatory change, we would look at our own resources first. Uh, and then we work with our monitor as well as to what that could involve. And it could maybe seconding people from other parts of government.

Uh, we don't rule out anything, so we haven't now spent a lot of time on that because it, it's really why would we, until we know we get some certainty. Yeah. We, we did do some early ball parking and I think we estimated around a million per year in the establishment phase that those figures are a couple of years old now.

So they may not be totally accurate, but it was sort of in that Yeah. Range Until we have more detail. So if there's a, there's a sort of a ballpark figure, but maybe a couple of years outta date now, is there a ballpark timeframe on on?

Well, I mean, we, well, If we're honest it, it's in advance. So we are not spending pretty much of our time, No, sorry. Not on the Oh, you mean not on the progression of the bill. Yeah. Um, we've stopped asking that question, but on the, um, on the ability for, um, BSA to be ready to go with people to be able to administer it. Yeah.

I think there were timeframes in the bill. It was around about a year for establishment. Yeah. And then another six months for having regulations in place. Or maybe it was the other way around. Uh, but there we were anticipating being able to meet those statutory timeframes. Yeah. But if we needed to stand it up quickly, we would Yeah.

Pretty, pretty nimble. Yeah. That would, uh, that would take a lot of focus, but we could do it. Yeah. But again, it's what, what what money comes with it. Thank you. Uh, Thank you. Um, I want to ask you about the, um, new streamlines of the streamlined way of dealing with complaints that I think came into effect, um, financial year 21 or 22.

Um, and how you've seen those now thinking through, they've had a little bit of time to bid in, but the information environment has even changed in that time, particularly public trust of New Zealanders. Yeah. Um, how are you balancing, uh, that, you know, the need to use taxpayers money wisely and to act in the public interest with that, particularly that accuracy test?

Um, when people are coming to you and saying, you know, a number of your complaints are around, um, the truth of climate change or the truth of, um, something which is presented as a scientific report. How are you balancing that now where people are getting their information from a range of sources, which is far outside of your purview? Well, I suppose the answer is we can only deal with a complaint when it comes in.

It must be about a broadcast. So there's a threshold and it must have first of all, gone to the broadcaster. So I don't know if this is quite answering you. So what we are seeing, and you mentioned accuracy, and it's interesting, we debate this ourselves. There is now a number of complaints where they are biases used.

Bias is not a standard and media are allowed to be biased. It's editorial discretion. Um, and usually it's tied up with really, it's an accuracy complaints or balance complaints. So you sort of triage them. And of course we do have the ability to add, uh, if we think it's in the interest of the complaint, that they really should have complained under balance.

Um, I think increasingly the, we do struggle to a degree. It's interesting. We have bias usually against all of you politicians. And it's usually one, we get them evenly balanced. So, so, so you know, that bias, that's not how it feels. But the trend currently seems to be what was not included and ought to have been included.

And that's, we say, well, that's not our, that that is editorial discretion. Now there are conceivable, there may be times where notwithstanding editorial, uh, discretion, there are matters that really ought to have been laid out a little more. But they are rare. They, they are rare.

And that's a struggle for us. We are not here to tell newsrooms particularly what to do. It's have they breached any of the standards. And, and those standards, you're quite right, do moves with societal norms and we hope that we reflect that. I don't think we are there to run ahead. We are to there to reflect societal norms.

Yes. If I can. Thank you. Um, I guess that's what I'm trying to get to. Those, um, streamlined processes that were developed, um, 21, 22, um, were in a period where, um, people's consumption of information was a bit different. Um, where people's, you know, new Zealanders perception of, um, things like climate change and um, efficacy of vaccines was, was rapidly changing because of the information environment that they were, um, in.

And now we have quite a different sort of political, uh, lens over a lot of that sort of information, which perhaps then was presented as sort of factually above the fright. So what I'm trying to understand is how do you make those calls when you are presented with, and I don't want to bring the committee into the actual decisions that you have made. 'cause those are, You can't, no, we can't. Those Are decisions.

Um, but there is a trend and that is the way that, um, most New Zealanders would engage with what you do and, and the value of your organization is those decisions. And many of them are about who's right, who is the narrator of this narrative. Yeah. I think it's about the content, if the content is correct or not. Yeah. Uh, according to, you know, who is viewing it.

Yeah, yeah. Correct. Kind of question. Yeah. Just in regards to those streamlined processes, I think, um, we might be referring to a time when, um, we had a high number of complaints, not, uh, the team wasn't as big as it, as huge as it is now. Um, and, and we had quite a backlog of complaints.

So we used those streamlined processes to manage our resources and clear that backlog. In the last couple of years, complaint numbers have come back down first to average levels. And actually in the financial year that's just ended, which is not yet under review. Uh, we only got 89 formal complaints, which is the first time we've had less than a hundred since the establishment years of the BSA in the early 19. That Might point to also what you're saying, that's what we are saying as well.

Yeah. So it points to a quite a mature co-regulatory system. It points to diminishing traditional broadcasting audiences, but it also points to, um, broadcasters, broadcasters understanding the standards and applying them well. Uh, and also dealing really well with the many complaints that they get. 'cause we know broadcasters get magnitudes more complaints than ever get referred to us.

And they treat people generally with a lot of care and respect. And so we don't see the vast majority of them, but it means that we don't need to run, uh, a streamlined process at the moment. Um, even a complaint which the authority declines to determine, which is one of its options under the act, still gets a written decision explaining why they've decline to determine the complaint.

So it's, um, yeah, it's, we are, we're still efficient, but we are not running a, um, yeah. As a sta helps. Yeah. Uh, new question. Yeah. New primary. Thank you. From 21, 22. Did you make any significant changes to the site during that time? No. I don't know that we even made any insignificant changes to the website during that time. However, we have made some changes since then and we're to make some came out and we're gonna make some more. What About our videos that we have put videos On? Um, in the last, uh, 12 months, we've created a series of, uh, videos that explain in really clear terms how to use our process, who can use our process.

Uh, and we've put those front and center on our home page, uh, have pretty good feedback. And actually our latest website ratings results were an improvement on the ones that you will have seen in the annual report that's currently under review. 'cause we're talking about sort of 18 months ago now. So, yeah. Yeah. We've had a bit of time to respond to those. Yeah, I appreciate That. Um, I mean, I had a look.

Couldn't see what there wants to complain about. Felt very clear. So, um, no complaint from me, just for the record, but, um, yeah, it is, and it explains, there's a lot of explanation in it, which is, but it's very well worded. Um, so is have you had any external consultation on why that might have been that people were unhappy with or reportedly unhappy with the website?

Have you? No. Um, but it sort of ties to dissatisfaction in other areas. I feel, um, you know, distrust in trust lower than distrust in media, distrust in public institutions, lower complainant satisfaction, sort, general disgruntlement. Um, it also could just be a blip.

Uh, so one, we, we haven't put too much stock in one year's worth of survey results. However, we have taken action to make the website a bit more friendly and, uh, approachable. 'cause we know that some of our resources were a bit, um, wordy, so try to simplify them a bit. Um, could I be bold and say that it could be relevant?

Yeah. How relevant are we to the youth? We're not. Mm-hmm. Okay. Um, New question. Yeah. Just wanna ask about the, um, uh, living income in this year. Um, and thinking about the way that it is, uh, at risk because of closures in the sector.

Um, how has that affected your financial planning this year? Um, with the closure of, I think it's, it was many weeks closure in the previous financial year to this one. Yeah. It's been so many. Yeah. And that, That would've, but that would've been, I guess, the big one that would've Yeah. Affected your financial performance this year. So can you talk through, um, you know, the way that you've responded to it in this year and how you intend to respond in the next couple of years?

Yeah. So we've been pretty conservative with our levy forecasts the last few years, knowing how challenging it is for local media at the moment. And, you know, some of the closures have resulted in gains for other media, others have not. So, uh, it's not easy to predict.

Um, and so far media have been doing pretty good job of adapting. Levies have been falling, but they've been falling slower than we've forecast. Uh, but we just continue to engage with broadcasters where we can, um, keep a pulse on who's doing what, but also just take a conservative approach and go what's probably the worst that could happen.

Mm-hmm. Is it just supplementary? Okay. Yeah. If, um, given, given the forecast decline, um, if it's not fair news, digital bargaining, um, how you've joined other, um, regulators overseas who are grappling with the same environment you are, um, does that look like the levy going up for the current, um, payers of that levy?

Does it look like fewer people in your organization able to respond to complaints? Does it look like complaints? Um, We're running, I think we're running, we run a very tight ship. Uh, we are running probably with one less staff member now, which we can manage, um, after we haven't declared what we have a, it's a better year than we had anticipated.

'cause it probably, uh, 'cause we are conservative, but I don't think there's anything but long term the levies will go down. There's fewer organizations. So it's each and every year. Or we do try and predict that you can't forecast out because look at the closures we've just had.

Mm-hmm. Um, but we are doing, working with a limited mandate and that's what we have to do. And we do carry quite heavy reserves. Um, but, uh, there'll be a time that that is not enough. But it's not here yet. The, Um, the Levy's formula is written into our act.

So we don't have the capacity to, uh, collect more if we need it on a, in a particular year. It's, it's very, And we control our expenses tightening. We're not extravagant. Um, obviously we're, we're interested 'cause we, we do often change the law. Um, but, um, help us to understand, um, you know, are there jurisdictions like ours that use, say private services to field complex?

Um, is, you mentioned contracting out, is this a service that doesn't have, um, government behind it in some jurisdictions? Um, no. Um, and so trying to understand how we make sure that people can still raise issues of accuracy and consistency offense. Well, it, first of all, yes, it has to be an understanding from any government that they want some regulator regulating these types of things. Mm-hmm. Broadcasting in its traditional sense has shrunk and is shrinking.

So it's, what is a broadcast or is it professional me news media, because most of the complaints are news. 'cause you feel A lot of experience.

Uh, secondly, focus primarily on content rather than platforms.
